sub_groups.name: Of the salvation of the gospel Joe 3:18; Zec 13:1; Re 7:17
The turgid, of the debasement of character Pr 25:26

ai_enrichment.summary: O conceito de 'fonte' na Bíblia é frequentemente utilizado de forma figurativa para representar a graça divina, a salvação e a degradação do caráter. Em Salmos 36:9, a fonte é associada à graça de Deus, enquanto em Jeremias 2:13, é vista como um símbolo de abandono da verdadeira fonte de água viva. Além disso, em passagens como Joel 3:18 e Zacarias 13:1, a fonte é ligada à salvação do evangelho. Por outro lado, Provérbios 25:26 menciona a fonte como uma metáfora para a degradação moral. Este tema revela a profundidade do simbolismo bíblico e sua relevância para a compreensão da relação entre Deus e a humanidade.
outline: A fonte como símbolo da graça divina
A fonte como representação da salvação no evangelho
A fonte como metáfora para a degradação do caráter
key_concepts: Graça divina como fonte de vida
ai_enrichment.theological_significance: O conceito de fonte é teologicamente significativo, pois ilustra a natureza de Deus como provedor de vida e salvação, contrastando com a condição humana de degradação moral. Essa dualidade enfatiza a necessidade de buscar a verdadeira fonte de vida em Deus, em vez de se desviar para fontes que não satisfazem.

definitions.text: (Heb. ‘ain; i.e., “eye” of the water desert), a natural source of living water. Palestine was a “land of brooks of water, of fountains, and depths that spring out of valleys and hills” (Deut. 8:7; 11:11). These fountains, bright sparkling “eyes” of the desert, are remarkable for their abundance and their beauty, especially on the west of Jordan. All the perennial rivers and streams of the country are supplied from fountains, and depend comparatively little on surface water. “Palestine is a country of mountains and hills, and it abounds in fountains of water. The murmur of these waters is heard in every dell, and the luxuriant foliage which surrounds them is seen in every plain.” Besides its rain-water, its cisterns and fountains, Jerusalem had also an abundant supply of water in the magnificent reservoir called “Solomon’s Pools” (q.v.), at the head of the Urtas valley, whence it was conveyed to the city by subterrean channels some 10 miles in length. These have all been long ago destroyed, so that no water from the “Pools” now reaches Jerusalem. Only one fountain has been discovered at Jerusalem, the so-called “Virgins’s Fountains,” in the valley of Kidron; and only one well (Heb. beer), the Bir Eyub, also in the valley of Kidron, south of the King’s Gardens, which has been dug through the solid rock. The inhabitants of Jerusalem are now mainly dependent on the winter rains, which they store in cisterns. (See WELL.)

(a spring in distinction from a well). The springs of Palestine, though short-lived, are remarkable for their abundance and beauty, especially those which fall into the Jordan and into its lakes, of which there are hundreds throughout its whole course. The spring or fountain of living water, the “eye” of the landscape, is distinguished in all Oriental languages from the artificially-sunk and enclosed well. Jerusalem appears to have possessed either more than one perennial spring or one issuing by more than one outlet. In Oriental cities generally public fountains are frequent. Traces of such fountains at Jerusalem may perhaps be found in the names of Enrogel, (2 Samuel 17:17) the “Dragon well” or fountain, and the “gate of the fountain.” (Nehemiah 2:13,14)

ai_themes_normalized.label_original: Graça divina como fonte de vida
